## [03:00 - 06:01]

and this is going to feed back into the whole concept of think different because Mulke also had another interesting quote where he said that all encounters with the enemy, I'm going to paraphrase again, excuse me, what he said was that all generals see the battlefield as a system of options. So in other words that first encounter, you know your initial battle plan that you plan for maybe you know days or weeks in advance, it doesn't survive that first encounter so the good generals are the ones who see that battlefield and then they say okay I can see how this is playing out, this is where I need to move the pieces correctly. So just like those great generals, the best tennis players, they've done that SWAT analysis and then once the match gets going they come back to DNO, they're like okay I've got this big toolbox of tactics I can use based on the evolution of the match so if you know plan A is not working, tactic A if that is not working I can go to tactic B, I can go to tactic C because again the tennis match is a series of options, it's a series of different tactics you can draw on to obviously you know turn things in your favor so with that in mind let's go to the other dry erase board and I'm going to diagram exactly how that works. To start let's see how this applies at the pro tour, the highest level of the game. We're going to look at the Roger Federer, Novak Djokovic semi-final match at the Aussie Open. Now what was the initial battle plan for both these guys? Well Federer was going to play aggressive first strike tennis, it's what he was doing at the end of last year under the tutelage of Paul Anacone, it's what he did over the course of the earlier rounds at the Aussie Open, he was having great success with just playing very aggressive tennis and very penetrating tennis, moving forward into the court. Now Djokovic he obviously knew that, he knew how Federer had been playing so what Djokovic wanted to do was play very solid defense, he wanted to hold Federer back, he wanted to force Federer to hit a series of big shots from behind the baseline to win a point and his hope there, his goal was that Federer wouldn't be able to string together a series of big shots, he would start making too many errors and that would allow Djokovic to win that match. So what happened in the first set and then early in the second set is first of all it was a tight match but Federer wasn't quite able to string together enough big shots to win the first and then in the second he actually got down a break and Djokovic was playing great defense, he was able to do exactly what he wanted to do which was to hold Federer behind the baseline because what Fed likes to do with that penetrating style of play he's really looking for a ball in the middle of the court here preferably on the forehand side if he gets that and he's inside the baseline the point's pretty much over, quick

## [06:01 - 09:01]

reminder by the way the circle has the player stick as the side they're heading on forehand for a righty, this is a backhand for a righty and it's reversed up there, this is a backhand for Djokovic, forehand for Djokovic. So this is what Federer was looking to do, move forward Djokovic did a great job holding Federer behind the baseline. So Federer knew this and Federer is saying to himself okay my initial battle plan didn't survive that first encounter so what adjustment can I make to specifically get some balls in this area of the court where I can continue to move forward and what's really important to understand is if your initial plan, your initial battle plan isn't working that doesn't mean you just completely take it and throw it out the window. So from the tennis perspective if your a counter puncher for example and your initial plan isn't working too well that doesn't mean you suddenly start to you know to become a serve and volleyer that's not going to work but it does make you make it, it does mean you make adjustments here and there and play within the framework of your overall game and what you're good at that's what these series of options really means in terms of the tennis match. So the adjustment Federer made was during the baseline rally when nobody had control Federer started throwing in more slices and some moon balls as well. So in other words he started throwing a lot of variety at Djokovic because previously like we said Federer was just trying to hit a bigger ball and that ball was a little bit more standardized a lot of pace but it allowed Djokovic to get grooved so Djokovic was able to play effective defense. But when Federer started throwing in some slice a little bit more air and then of course the aggressively hit balls as well Djokovic had you know his timing went off a little bit so not only did he make some errors just gave Fed some free points he also left some ball short so then Federer was able to step in he was able to penetrate the court more which had been his overarching strategy coming into the match and again once he's in here then he's really you know he's in control of the point despite how good you know despite Djokovic's defense which is phenomenal you give Federer a ball inside the baseline he's going to win that point most of the time. So that adjustment worked and like I said before Federer was down a break early in the second he actually ended up going up 5-2 in the second set but then for whatever reason Federer decided to kind of go back to his old strategy which is just sort of hit big off the ground he kind of got away from that variety then Djokovic won the second set came back and then he won then he won the third set obviously winning in straight sets. Now what's really you know important to highlight here is that the tennis a tennis match is a troubleshooting exercise it's not like these guys come into the match and they say okay if plan you know if my opponent does this I'm going to do this but if my opponent does this I'm going to do this and so on. They're making adjustments on the fly they're looking at the battlefield and they're saying

okay this is what's going on I've evaluated the situation correctly now this is what I need to do.
